summary: Gruber reverses his initial dismissal of the Guardian's report that Apple contractors were listening to Siri recordings, calling it a genuine privacy fiasco and betrayal of user trust. He criticizes Apple for saving recordings without explicit consent, keeping identifiable copies for six months, and offering no user controls — noting that even Amazon provides better transparency with Alexa. He argues that accidental Siri invocations should be deleted immediately and that Apple should adopt an explicit per-interaction opt-in model, similar to its own voicemail transcription feature. He concludes by invoking Steve Jobs's own words about asking users repeatedly for consent in plain English, highlighting Apple's failure to live up to its own stated principles.
key_points: Apple's Siri grading program recorded users without explicit consent, contradicting the company's privacy-first branding
Amazon actually provides better privacy transparency for Alexa than Apple does for Siri, with clear settings and the ability to review recordings
Apple's own voicemail transcription feature demonstrates exactly the right model: explicit, per-interaction consent with clear language
Accidental Siri invocations — when the device mishears 'Hey Siri' — represent the nightmare scenario and should be deleted immediately
Apple needs to stop pretending Siri is perfect and let users voluntarily submit failed interactions to improve the system
money_quotes: This is a privacy fiasco, and a betrayal of Siri users' trust.
If I graded Apple on the privacy and trust implications of this, I'd give them an F.
The billboards should have read 'What happens on your iPhone stays on your iPhone, except for some of your Siri recordings, which we listen to.'
Having Apple contractors listen to random conversations or audio is the nightmare scenario for an always-listening voice assistant.
Until the opt-in process is crystal clear, Apple should delete all existing recordings and confirm that it is no longer saving them.
We should expect Apple to lead the industry on this front, but in fact, they're far behind.
Apple needs to stop pretending Siri is perfect.
Privacy means people know what they're signing up for, in plain English and repeatedly.
key_insight: Apple's Siri recording practices violated the very privacy principles the company built its brand on, and the fix is simple: explicit, per-interaction user consent — a model Apple already uses for voicemail transcription.
